title: Listening - Part 1
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1 – 6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two people discussing the benefits of 'binaural beats' for concentration.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I’ve started listening to these 'binaural beats' while I study. It’s supposed to sync your brain waves. Woman: [1]I tried that during my finals, but I found the constant humming a bit too intrusive[/1] to actually focus. Man: Really? I find it helps block out the background noise of the library perfectly. Woman: [2]There’s still a lot of debate in the scientific community about whether it’s just a placebo effect[/2], you know. Man: Even if it is, if I feel like I’m concentrating better, does it really matter? Woman: I suppose not. But I’ll stick to my classical music. It’s a bit more melodious at least.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I’ve started listening to these 'binaural beats' while I study. It’s supposed to sync your brain waves.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I tried that during my finals, but I found the constant humming a bit too intrusive to actually focus.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Really? I find it helps block out the background noise of the library perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: There’s still a lot of debate in the scientific community about whether it’s just a placebo effect, you know.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Even if it is, if I feel like I’m concentrating better, does it really matter?

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I suppose not. But I’ll stick to my classical music. It’s a bit more melodious at least.

questions.question: What was the woman's personal experience with binaural beats?

options.A: She found them more effective than music.
options.B: She was distracted by the nature of the sound.
options.C: She was unable to hear them properly.

questions.explanation: She describes the 'constant humming' as 'too intrusive', which means it was distracting/annoying.

questions.question: What point does the woman make about the technology?

options.A: Its benefits have been conclusively proven.
options.B: It is potentially harmful to long-term hearing.
options.C: Its actual efficacy is currently unverified.

questions.explanation: She mentions a 'debate' about whether it is a 'placebo effect', implying the actual scientific efficacy is not yet certain.

extracts.context: You hear two friends, Connor and Emily, discussing a local 'repair café'.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I finally got my old record player fixed at that repair café in the community centre. Woman: [3]It’s a brilliant initiative, isn't it? It’s so much more sustainable than just buying new electronics[/3]. Man: It is, though I was a bit embarrassed when it turned out the 'broken' part was just a loose wire I could have fixed myself. Woman: [4]The volunteers there are great, though—they actually show you how to do it so you're better prepared next time[/4]. Man: That was the best part. It’s not just a service; it’s like a mini-workshop. Woman: Exactly. We need more of that kind of community-led skill sharing.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I finally got my old record player fixed at that repair café in the community centre.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: It’s a brilliant initiative, isn't it? It’s so much more sustainable than just buying new electronics.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: It is, though I was a bit embarrassed when it turned out the 'broken' part was just a loose wire I could have fixed myself.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: The volunteers there are great, though—they actually show you how to do it so you're better prepared next time.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: That was the best part. It’s not just a service; it’s like a mini-workshop.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Exactly. We need more of that kind of community-led skill sharing.

questions.question: What do both speakers agree is a major advantage of the repair café?

options.A: The professional quality of the repairs.
options.C: The low cost of the spare parts.

questions.explanation: Emily calls it 'sustainable' (environmentally friendly) and Connor agrees ('It is').

questions.question: What particular aspect of the café did the man find valuable?

options.A: The opportunity to acquire new practical skills.
options.B: The social atmosphere of the community centre.
options.C: The speed at which his device was repaired.

questions.explanation: He calls it a 'mini-workshop' where they 'show you how to do it', referring to skill acquisition.

extracts.context: You hear two people discussing the importance of 'soft skills' in the modern workplace.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I see the new management trainee program is focusing heavily on emotional intelligence this year. Woman: About time! [5]You can teach anyone to use a software package, but you can't easily teach someone how to handle a difficult client[/5]. Man: I agree, though [6]I worry that some of the 'empathy training' exercises are becoming a bit box-ticking[/6]. They feel a bit performative. Woman: I know what you mean. But at least the conversation is happening. In the past, those 'human' elements were completely ignored. Man: True. It’s definitely a shift in the right direction, even if the execution is sometimes a bit clumsy.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I see the new management trainee program is focusing heavily on emotional intelligence this year.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: About time! You can teach anyone to use a software package, but you can't easily teach someone how to handle a difficult client.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I agree, though I worry that some of the 'empathy training' exercises are becoming a bit box-ticking. They feel a bit performative.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I know what you mean. But at least the conversation is happening. In the past, those 'human' elements were completely ignored.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: True. It’s definitely a shift in the right direction, even if the execution is sometimes a bit clumsy.

questions.question: The woman believes that interpersonal skills are...

options.B: more difficult to acquire than technical ones.

questions.explanation: She says you can teach software (technical) easily, but not how to handle a difficult client (interpersonal), implying the latter is harder to teach/acquire.

questions.question: What is the man's concern about the current approach to training?

options.A: It is too expensive for small companies.
options.B: It lacks a practical, real-world application.
options.C: It may be lacking in genuine sincerity.

questions.explanation: He calls it 'box-ticking' and 'performative', suggesting it isn't sincere or deeply felt.
